objections.description: The SourceRouter conflates two distinct concerns: (1) where to discover edition metadata and (2) where to download the ZIP/XML payload. INLABS serves both an API for discovery (listing editions) and a download endpoint. Liferay serves a JSONWS catalog AND hosts ZIPs. The proposed resolve_sources() returns a flat list[Source] without distinguishing discovery vs download phases. This means the downloader will try to 'download from INLABS' even when only the catalog was needed from Liferay, or vice-versa. In the edge case where INLABS discovery succeeds but download fails (e.g., rate limit), the fallback list doesn't know to try Liferay download with the same edition metadata. This is a design error that will produce silent data gaps in the 25-35 day overlap window.
objections.suggested_fix: Split SourceRouter into two explicit phases: resolve_discovery_sources(edition_date) -> list[DiscoverySource] and resolve_download_sources(edition_date, edition_metadata) -> list[DownloadSource]. DiscoverySource provides catalog/listing endpoints. DownloadSource provides the actual ZIP/XML URL. The downloader receives the resolved metadata from discovery and independently resolves download sources. This also cleanly handles the case where INLABS discovery works but download is throttled — you fall back to Liferay download using the same edition identifiers.

objections.description: The proposed ID scheme sha256(f'{edition_date}|{section}|{page}|{ordinal}') is fragile. The DOU can republish or rectify atos, changing content on the same page/ordinal. Worse, 'ordinal' is an extraction artifact — if the XML parser changes order or the DOU restructures a section, every ID shifts. This makes the UNIQUE constraint a correctness hazard: legitimate updated atos get silently dropped by INSERT OR IGNORE. The artifact doesn't address republication/retificação at all, which is a known DOU pattern.
objections.suggested_fix: Use content-addressing for deduplication but identity-addressing for the primary key. Primary ID: sha256(f'{edition_date}|{section}|{act_type}|{act_number}') where act_number is the official ato identifier (e.g., 'PORTARIA Nº 123'). Dedup hash (separate column): sha256(normalized_body_text). On conflict of primary ID, UPDATE the record (upsert) rather than IGNORE — this handles retificações. On conflict of dedup hash across different primary IDs, flag as potential duplicate for review. Add a 'version' counter column that increments on upsert.

objections.description: The state machine shows 'RETRY_PENDING → estado anterior (re-entry)' but doesn't specify WHERE the state is stored atomically with the retry metadata. In SQLite with WAL, if the worker crashes between writing RETRY_PENDING and writing next_attempt_at, the entry is stuck in RETRY_PENDING forever with no scheduled re-attempt. The watchdog stale-check (>30min) won't catch this because RETRY_PENDING isn't listed as a stale-eligible state — only DOWNLOADING/EXTRACTING/INGESTING are. This is a liveness bug.
objections.suggested_fix: All state transitions including retry metadata (retry_count, next_attempt_at, last_error) must be written in a single UPDATE statement — no multi-step writes. Add RETRY_PENDING to the watchdog stale-check with a separate threshold: if next_attempt_at < now() - 5min AND state = RETRY_PENDING, treat as stuck and either re-trigger or escalate to FAILED. Document the exact SQL: UPDATE editions SET state='RETRY_PENDING', retry_count=retry_count+1, next_attempt_at=?, last_error=? WHERE id=? AND state=?

objections.location: Section P1.2 — ES mapping: dense_vector dims=1024 hardcoded
objections.description: Hardcoding dims=1024 with index=false is presented as 'zero cost' but it's not. The field exists in the mapping and will cause mapping conflicts when the actual model is chosen. If dims differ (768 for multilingual-e5-base, 384 for MiniLM), you cannot change dims on an existing mapping — you must reindex. Meanwhile, a dense_vector field with index=false still occupies mapping metadata and complicates index template management. The 'zero cost' claim is misleading.
objections.suggested_fix: Do NOT add the dense_vector field to v1 mapping at all. Instead, document the embedding strategy in an ADR (Architecture Decision Record) and plan for index aliasing: v1 index has no vector field, v2 (created when model is chosen) adds the correct dims. Use index aliases (dou-read, dou-write) from day one so the reindex + alias swap is seamless. This is genuinely zero cost and avoids a guaranteed future reindex caused by guessing dims wrong.

objections.location: Section P1.3 — Watchdog: Missing edition check 'dia útil sem DOU até 10h BRT'
objections.description: The watchdog's 'missing edition' check assumes DOU publishes every business day, but DOU has known exceptions: optional holidays (ponto facultativo), delayed publications, and extraordinary editions (edição extra) that publish on weekends. A naive 'is_business_day()' check will generate false-positive alerts on pontos facultativos and miss extraordinary editions entirely. The artifact doesn't specify the holiday calendar source or how to handle edições extras.
objections.suggested_fix: Use a conservative approach: (1) maintain a minimal holiday list (feriados nacionais only — ~12 dates/year, easily hardcoded and updated annually), (2) for the missing-edition check, use a 2-hour grace period past the typical publication time (DOU usually publishes by 6h BRT, alert at 12h not 10h), (3) for extraordinary editions, the discovery phase should check INLABS with section filter for 'extra' editions on every run regardless of day-of-week, (4) alerts for missing editions should be WARNING not CRITICAL — a human should confirm before escalating.

objections.location: Section P0.1 — Downloader: disk space pre-check
objections.description: The 500MB minimum free disk check is a point-in-time check that's vulnerable to TOCTOU: another process (or concurrent extraction) can consume disk between the check and the download. On Fly.io with a shared volume, this is not theoretical — the extractor decompressing a previous edition's ZIP while the downloader starts a new one creates exactly this race.
objections.suggested_fix: Keep the pre-check but add a secondary guard: the streaming download should check available disk every N chunks (e.g., every 50MB written) and abort with a clean FAILED transition if free space drops below 200MB. Additionally, the pipeline should be strictly sequential per-edition (which the artifact implies but doesn't enforce): download → extract → ingest → cleanup before starting the next edition. Enforce this with a single-flight lock in the scheduler.

objections.location: Section 5 — Sprint ordering: P1.5 before P1.1
objections.description: The sprint ordering puts SQLite PRAGMAs (P1.5) first, but the PRAGMA changes require testing under concurrency. Without the deterministic ID scheme (P1.1) and the retry columns (P1.4), there's nothing meaningful to test concurrent writes against. More critically, P1.2 (ES mapping) is in Sprint 2 but P0.2 (SourceRouter) is in Sprint 1 — the SourceRouter records 'source' in the registry, which depends on the schema having a 'source' column, which is defined in P1.2. Dependency chain is broken.
objections.suggested_fix: Reorder Sprint 1: (1) Schema migration first — add all new columns (deterministic_id, source, retry_count, last_error, next_attempt_at) + PRAGMAs in a single migration script, (2) P1.1 deterministic IDs, (3) P0.1 downloader streaming, (4) P0.2 SourceRouter, (5) P1.4 retry. Move the 'source' column addition out of P1.2 (ES mapping) into the schema migration. P1.2 should only cover ES-side concerns.

suggestions.description: The @retryable decorator approach is clean but consider making it state-machine-aware rather than a generic decorator. The retry should persist the attempt to SQLite BEFORE sleeping for backoff — if the worker is killed during backoff sleep, the retry_count is lost and resets to 0 on restart. Use a 'retry loop' pattern that reads next_attempt_at from the registry and skips entries whose next_attempt_at > now(), rather than an in-process sleep.

Rather than a badge saying 'Read-Only Dashboard', enforce it architecturally: the dashboard should query a read-only SQLite replica (using Litestream or Fly.io's built-in LiteFS) or at minimum a separate connection with ?mode=ro. The worker API should not even HAVE mutation endpoints that the dashboard could theoretically hit. If there's no mutation endpoint, there's no auth decision to make for the dashboard.

suggestions.location: Section 6 — Risk R3: ES single-node snapshots
suggestions.description: ES snapshots to S3/R2 require the repository-s3 plugin and credentials management. A simpler resilience strategy for a single-node setup: since SQLite is the source of truth and ES is a derived index, ensure the ingestor can fully rebuild the ES index from SQLite + stored XMLs (or re-downloaded ZIPs). Document the rebuild procedure and test it. This is cheaper and more reliable than managing ES snapshot infrastructure for a single-node deployment.

Promote this to P1 tier. Without structured logging with correlation IDs, debugging any of the P0/P1 issues in production on Fly.io will be done by grepping unstructured text in `fly logs`. Every P0/P1 implementation will benefit from having structured logging in place first. It's 2-3 hours of work and should be item 0 in Sprint 1.
